title: Bubble Sort
description:
Sort an array of N integers in ascending order using Bubble Sort.
Print the sorted array space-separated.

Key Interview Facts:
• Stable sort algorithm
• Time: O(N²) worst/average, O(N) best (already sorted with flag)
• Space: O(1) in-place

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Compare adjacent pairs and swap if out of order.
After each full pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles' to its correct position.
Optimize with an early exit flag — if no swaps in a pass, array is sorted.

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
# Bubble Sort with optimization flag
for i in range(n-1):
    swapped = False
    # inner pass
print(*arr)
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Repeatedly pass through the array, swapping adjacent elements if out of order. After each pass, the next largest element is in its final position. Early exit flag improves best case to O(N).

title: Merge Sort
description:
Sort an array of N integers using Merge Sort. Print sorted array.

Key Interview Facts:
• Stable sort algorithm ✓
• Time: O(N log N) — always
• Space: O(N) — extra array needed
• Preferred for linked lists and external sorting

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Divide array into two halves.
Recursively sort each half.
Merge the two sorted halves in O(N) time.

solutions.python:
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    L = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    R = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    res, i, j = [], 0, 0
    while i < len(L) and j < len(R):
        if L[i] <= R[j]: res.append(L[i]); i+=1
        else: res.append(R[j]); j+=1
    return res + L[i:] + R[j:]
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(*merge_sort(arr))

starterCode.python:
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    L = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    R = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    # Merge L and R
    pass
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
print(*merge_sort(arr))
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ static void mergeSort(int[] a, int l, int r){ if(l>=r) return; } public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); mergeSort(a,0,n-1); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Divide array in half, recursively sort each, then merge two sorted halves in O(N). Guaranteed O(N log N) — unlike Quick Sort which can degrade to O(N²) in worst case. Merge Sort is stable.

title: Quick Sort
description:
Sort an array of N integers using Quick Sort. Print sorted array.

Key Interview Facts:
• NOT stable (elements with equal keys may change order)
• Average Time: O(N log N) — preferred in practice
• Worst Time: O(N²) — when pivot is always min/max (rare with good pivot strategy)
• Space: O(log N) — recursive call stack

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Choose a pivot (last element is simplest).
Partition: all elements < pivot go left, > pivot go right.
Recursively sort left and right partitions.

solutions.python:
def quick_sort(arr, lo, hi):
    if lo >= hi: return
    pivot = arr[hi]
    i = lo - 1
    for j in range(lo, hi):
        if arr[j] <= pivot:
            i += 1
            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
    arr[i+1], arr[hi] = arr[hi], arr[i+1]
    p = i + 1
    quick_sort(arr, lo, p-1)
    quick_sort(arr, p+1, hi)
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
quick_sort(arr, 0, n-1)
print(*arr)

starterCode.python:
def partition(arr, lo, hi):
    pivot = arr[hi]
    i = lo - 1
    # Partition logic
    return i + 1

def quick_sort(arr, lo, hi):
    if lo >= hi: return
    p = partition(arr, lo, hi)
    # Recurse

n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
quick_sort(arr, 0, n-1)
print(*arr)
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ static void quickSort(int[] a, int lo, int hi){ if(lo>=hi) return; } public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); quickSort(a,0,n-1); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Quick Sort partitions array around a pivot. Elements ≤ pivot go left, others go right. Pivot is then in its final position. Recursively sort left and right. Average O(N log N), but O(N²) worst case. Preferred over Merge Sort for in-place sorting due to better cache performance.
timeComplexity: O(N log N) average, O(N²) worst

title: Frequency Count
description:
Given an array of N integers, print the frequency of each distinct element in sorted order of elements.
Format: "element: count" per line.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Use a HashMap/dictionary to count occurrences.
Sort by element and print.

solutions.python:
from collections import Counter
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
for k, v in sorted(Counter(arr).items()):
    print(f'{k}: {v}')

starterCode.python:
from collections import Counter
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
# Count and print in sorted order
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); TreeMap<Integer,Integer> freq=new TreeMap<>(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Use a HashMap to count occurrences in O(N). Then sort keys (or use TreeMap/OrderedDict) and print each element with its count.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
description:
Find the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence in an unsorted array.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N integers
hints: Put all elements in a HashSet.
For each element x, check if x-1 is NOT in the set (i.e., x is a sequence start).
From each sequence start, extend the sequence and track length.

solutions.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
num_set = set(arr)
best = 0
for x in num_set:
    if x - 1 not in num_set:
        cur = x
        length = 1
        while cur + 1 in num_set:
            cur += 1
            length += 1
        best = max(best, length)
print(best)

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
num_set = set(arr)
best = 0
# For each sequence start, extend and track length
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); Set<Integer> s=new HashSet<>(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Use a HashSet for O(1) lookup. For each element, only start counting from it if it's a sequence start (x-1 not in set). Total work is O(N) since each element is visited once.

title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal
description:
Given a binary tree as an array representation (1-indexed, -1 means null),
print the inorder traversal (Left, Root, Right) space-separated.

Input:
Line 1: N (number of nodes)
Line 2: N values (-1 = null)
hints: Inorder: recursively traverse left, visit root, traverse right.
Array representation: left child = 2*i, right child = 2*i+1 (1-indexed).

solutions.python:
n = int(input())
arr = [0] + list(map(int, input().split()))  # 1-indexed
result = []
def inorder(i):
    if i > n or arr[i] == -1: return
    inorder(2*i)
    result.append(arr[i])
    inorder(2*i+1)
inorder(1)
print(*result)

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = [0] + list(map(int, input().split()))
result = []
def inorder(i):
    if i > n or arr[i] == -1: return
    # Left, Root, Right
print(*result)
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ static void inorder(int[] arr,int i,int n,List<Integer> res){ if(i>n||arr[i]==-1) return; } public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] arr=new int[n+1]; for(int i=1;i<=n;i++) arr[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Inorder traversal visits Left subtree → Root → Right subtree. For BSTs, this gives elements in sorted order. Array representation: node i has left child 2i, right child 2i+1.

title: Height of a Binary Tree
description:
Given a binary tree as an array (1-indexed, -1 = null), find its height (max depth).
Root is at depth 1.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N values
hints: Height = 1 + max(height(left), height(right))
Base case: null node has height 0.

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = [0] + list(map(int, input().split()))
def height(i):
    if i > n or arr[i] == -1: return 0
    # Return 1 + max(left height, right height)
print(height(1))
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ static int height(int[] a,int i,int n){ if(i>n||a[i]==-1) return 0; return 0; } public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n+1]; for(int i=1;i<=n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); System.out.println(height(a,1,n)); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: Recursively compute height of left and right subtrees. Height of a node = 1 + max(leftHeight, rightHeight). Height of null = 0. This produces the maximum depth of the tree.

title: House Robber
description:
You are a robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has some amount of money.
Adjacent houses have security systems — you CANNOT rob two adjacent houses.
Find the maximum amount you can rob.

Input:
Line 1: N
Line 2: N non-negative integers (money in each house)
hints: dp[i] = max money from first i houses.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + money[i])
Either skip current house or rob it (and skip previous).

starterCode.python:
n = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
# dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + arr[i])
# Use space-optimized two variables
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(); int[] a=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) a[i]=sc.nextInt(); } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n; scanf("%d",&n); return 0; }

explanation: DP recurrence: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + arr[i]). Either skip house i (take dp[i-1]) or rob house i but skip i-1 (take dp[i-2] + arr[i]). Space-optimized to O(1) with two variables.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
description:
Find the length of the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) of two strings.
A subsequence is formed by deleting characters (order preserved, not necessarily contiguous).

Input:
Line 1: string s1
Line 2: string s2
hints: Build a 2D DP table dp[i][j] = LCS of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1].
Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])

starterCode.python:
s1 = input().strip()
s2 = input().strip()
m, n = len(s1), len(s2)
dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]
# Fill dp table
print(dp[m][n])
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); String s1=sc.next(),s2=sc.next(); } }

explanation: Build a 2D DP table. dp[i][j] = LCS of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]. If characters match, extend by 1. Otherwise, take the max from left or top. Final answer is dp[m][n].

title: Coin Change – Minimum Coins
description:
Given coin denominations and an amount, find the minimum number of coins to make that amount.
If impossible, print -1.

Input:
Line 1: N (number of coin types) and amount
Line 2: N coin denominations
hints: dp[a] = min coins needed to make amount a.
For each coin, dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin] + 1)
Initialize dp[0] = 0, dp[i>0] = infinity.

solutions.python:
line = input().split()
n, amount = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
coins = list(map(int, input().split()))
INF = float('inf')
dp = [INF] * (amount + 1)
dp[0] = 0
for a in range(1, amount + 1):
    for c in coins:
        if c <= a and dp[a-c] + 1 < dp[a]:
            dp[a] = dp[a-c] + 1
print(-1 if dp[amount] == INF else dp[amount])

starterCode.python:
line = input().split()
n, amount = int(line[0]), int(line[1])
coins = list(map(int, input().split()))
dp = [float('inf')] * (amount + 1)
dp[0] = 0
# For each amount a, try each coin
print(-1 if dp[amount] == float('inf') else dp[amount])
starterCode.java:
import java.util.*;
public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){ Scanner sc=new Scanner(System.in); int n=sc.nextInt(),amount=sc.nextInt(); int[] coins=new int[n]; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) coins[i]=sc.nextInt(); int[] dp=new int[amount+1]; Arrays.fill(dp,amount+1); dp[0]=0; } }
starterCode.c:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){ int n,amount; scanf("%d %d",&n,&amount); return 0; }

explanation: Bottom-up DP: dp[a] = minimum coins to make amount a. For each sub-amount, try all coins and take the minimum. dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin] + 1). Classic unbounded knapsack variant.
